Everything lands in one place, ready for the writer to start immediately.

content:
India makes over a billion business calls every day. Most of them are still handled by humans.

One Bengaluru startup just raised $6.3 million to change that.

Meet Bolna (YC F25) AI - built by Maitreya Wagh (ex-Bain) and Prateek Sachan (ex-Zomato).

They noticed India never moved to chat and email the way the rest of the world did. Bank collections, lead qualification, delivery confirmations - it all still runs on humans. 

But building voice AI for India turned out to be harder than anywhere else. Here's what they figured out:

1. India's language problem is unlike any other

An Indian caller will open in Hindi, mix in English, and slip into their dialect - all in one sentence. Bolna built for 10+ Indian languages, including mid-conversation switching.

2. No single AI model is good at everything

OpenAI handles reasoning well. But for Indian accents and dialects, Sarvam AI - an Indian company that built its models specifically for Indian languages - does it better. Bolna automatically picks between them on every call.

3. It has to be cheap - or it’ll never scale

They’re priced very competitively at ₹7/min, dropping to ₹3/min at scale. That math only works if you're not burning expensive AI on every call - which they aren't.

The traction has been hard to ignore:

- $6.3 million raised
- 1,050+ paying customers
- 1,500 to 200,000 calls per day in under 8 months

YC once told them "Indian enterprises won't pay for this." 

The founders proved them wrong, applied 5 times, and got accepted as well.

Do you think voice will outpace text as India's primary AI interface?

content:
Most people trying to sell AI automation will make $0.

Because businesses don’t pay for “AI agents.” They pay for workflows that save time or make money.

And these are the 5 workflows they keep buying:

▶️ 1. Instant lead response systems

↳ A lead fills out a form on a website. Within seconds, they get an automated WhatsApp or email asking qualifying questions and the sales rep gets notified with all the details.

↳ Best for: Dental clinics, real estate, law firms, agencies.

▶️ 2. Invoice and document processing

↳ Invoices arrive in a company inbox as PDFs. AI reads them, extracts the vendor name, amount, and date, and pushes that data straight into their accounting software or CRM without manual data entry.

↳ Best for: Accounting firms, insurance, logistics, legal teams.

 ▶️ 3. Follow-up and nurture sequences

↳ Someone attends a webinar or fills an enquiry form but never books a call. The system automatically sends personalised WhatsApp or email follow-ups over the next few days until they either reply, book, or opt out.

↳ Best for: Coaches, consultants, agencies, SaaS, B2B service providers.

▶️ 4. Database reactivation

↳ Businesses usually have thousands of dead leads sitting inside CRMs. AI sends personalised outreach referencing their previous interaction and interested leads are automatically flagged for the sales team.

↳ Best for: Gyms, SaaS, e-commerce, dental clinics, subscription businesses.

▶️ 5. Automated reporting dashboards

↳ Instead of someone manually pulling numbers from their CRM, ad dashboard, and spreadsheets every morning - this workflow does it automatically and delivers a clean daily or weekly summary straight to email.

↳ Best for: Agencies, sales teams, startups, any company running multiple tools.

The best part?

Every single one of these can be built with n8n or Make.

→ Use n8n if you want lower costs and don't mind a slightly steeper learning curve.

→ Use Make.com if you want to land your first client fast and learn as you go.

Which of these 5 would you build first?

We integrated AI into every stage of our production process. It cut our turnaround time in half - without replacing a single person.

At one point, every video was taking almost 30 days to go from idea to upload. This stopped us from reaching our goal of hitting 220 million views in 2025.

So instead of telling the team to “work harder” this year, we audited the entire production pipeline to figure out where the time was actually going.

Here's what we found:

→ Research: writers spending 3+ hours per case before writing a single word

→ Subtitles: QA team spending a full day per video checking and correcting captions

→ Thumbnails: our artist spending half a day on research before design even started

→ Quality checks: multiple rounds of manual review at every stage

Each stage had its own bottleneck. And each one was eating days we didn't have.

So we built AI tools to fix each one:

-A research tool that pulls full case details, court dates, and footage timestamps automatically

-A subtitle engine built on Gemini API that hits 93-94% accuracy - more than the ones in the market

-A thumbnail tool that handles all research and title generation before the artist opens Photoshop

-A QA tool that cuts review time from a full day to half a day per video

The result: 30 days down to 15 days per video.
Same team. No new hires. Just a faster system.

I think a lot of founders misunderstand how AI changes a business.

The biggest advantage isn’t replacing people. It’s helping them spend less time on repetitive work that slows everything down.

Because most delays in a company come from 50 small tasks that quietly eat a few extra hours every single day.

We ditched every subtitle tool in the market - even the ones that hit 85% accuracy.

That sounds like overkill, until you realise that for us, a wrong word in a subtitle isn't just an editing error. It can completely change the meaning of what someone said. 

We couldn't afford that risk. So we built our own subtitle tool and got it to 93–94% accuracy in one week.

Here’s how:

We started with Whisper AI because it's what most production companies use locally.

Spent weeks testing it. Different prompts. Different workflows. Different pipelines. But couldn’t push it beyond 80–85% consistently.

Which meant: 15–20 wrong words every 100 words.

At scale, that became a massive editing bottleneck for our QA team.

So instead of forcing the tool to work, we rebuilt the system using Gemini API. The difference was impressive.

But accuracy was only part of the problem.

Every subtitle tool formats captions differently:

-Random line breaks
-Inconsistent timing
-Subtitles that look terrible on mobile

So we built a standardisation layer on top of the AI:

→ Fixed character limits per line
→ Balanced timing between cuts
→ Controlled line breaks for readability
→ Standardised caption sizing across formats

This is why we invest in building our own tools. The market wasn't solving our problem. So we did. 

Every video that goes out now saves our QA team half a day of work.

Have you ever built something in-house because existing tools weren’t good enough?

A guy with zero coding experience built an AI SaaS that automates dental diagnosis reports. And made over $100K from it.

Meet Ben. He's not a developer. Didn't know how to code before starting. He just saw a painful problem in dentistry and decided to figure it out anyway.

Here's exactly how he did it:

▶️ 1. Stopped chasing startup ideas 

Instead of inventing the "next big app," he looked for industries drowning in repetitive manual work. 

Dentistry kept coming up. Clinics were spending hours manually writing treatment plans and diagnosis reports. That became the opportunity.

▶️ 2. Validated the problem before building anything

Most people spend months building products nobody asked for. He did the opposite.

He cold called dentists and asked one simple question: "If this existed, would you actually use it?" Those conversations shaped the entire product.

▶️ 3. Built only the core output first

He didn’t go for any fancy dashboard, polished UI or complex features.

Just: upload X-ray, AI analyses it, treatment report gets generated. That was enough to test if it solved a real problem.

▶️ 4. He used AI tools to replace an engineering team

His stack cost less than $100/month:

→ Cursor for vibe coding
→ GPT/Claude for debugging
→ Lovable for UI generation
→ Make.com for automation
→ Supabase for database
→ Render for deployment

▶️ 5. He learned while building

He didn't disappear for two years to "become technical." He broke things, fixed bugs, watched tutorials, asked AI dumb questions, and repeated that every single day.

That's the part most people skip.

The barrier to building software is no longer coding - it’s finding a painful problem and having the courage to start.

If you understand the problem deeply, today AI can help you build the solution faster than ever.

content:
A mechanical engineer with no MBA just became CEO of the world's most valuable company.

Here's what John Ternus did differently to climb to the top of Apple:

1. Didn't just focus on academics in college

He competed in swimming while studying mechanical engineering at UPenn. It took focus away from his studies at times, but it built discipline in a way a classroom never could.

2. Started solving real problems as a student

For his final year project, he built a mechanical feeding arm that people with quadriplegia could control using head movements. Gave himself real-world experience.

3. Chose a small company over a big name

After graduating, he joined a small startup building VR headsets instead of chasing a brand-name employer. Smaller companies move faster, and he could learn without waiting for approvals.

4. Never said no to boring work

Then, he joined Apple, where his first task was counting the grooves on a screw head with a magnifying glass. He thought “Is this normal?” - but he did it anyway.

5. Spent a decade in the background

For over 10 years, he quietly with no public visibility. He was absorbing Apple's culture - the perfectionism, the focus on quality.

6. Never asked for responsibility - just kept earning it

In 2020, he was handed iPhone hardware engineering - Apple's biggest business at $200B+ a year. It was a scary role, but he took it on and made it work.

7. Became the youngest on Apple's executive team

And led the Mac's full transition from Intel to Apple Silicon. One of the most complex engineering bets in Apple's history. 

8. Stayed with one company for 25 years

Learning from the best, and quietly building trust from the inside. 

That's how a mechanical engineer with no MBA ends up running a $4 trillion company.

As someone who’s an engineer-turned-CEO too, I love this story.

My degree didn't build NB Media. But the problems I tried solving, my businesses that failed, the skills I learnt along the way… they all did.

Degrees open doors. But they don't build companies.

What do you think of his journey?

#apple #ceo #leadership

This IIT student turned down $525,000 and a Stanford PhD to build his own startup in his dorm room.

Varun grew up with very limited means, often struggling to make ends meet. But he went on to crack IIT Kharagpur - and later won over $100,000 in Kaggle coding competitions.

content:
This founder raised $21 million, had to shut down his own company overnight, and rebuilt from scratch.

Today, his product is used by Fortune 500 companies, and he's growing 40% month on month.

Meet Tanay Kothari - founder of Wispr Flow, an AI tool that turns your natural speech into formatted text directly inside any app.

Here's how he did it:

1. Grew up chasing one idea

Growing up in Delhi, Tanay had the idea to build tech that felt as natural as talking to a close friend. That obsession eventually took him to Stanford.

2. Tried building it before the world was ready

At 18, he built one of the earliest voice assistants - before Siri and Alexa became mainstream. It hit 2.5 million users in 6 months. But Google shut it down with a cease-and-desist.

3. Raised $21 million for a different idea

Years later, Tanay and his Stanford roommate raised money to build a Bluetooth earpiece that could convert silent speech into text without saying a word out loud.

4. Found out he was building the wrong thing

Tanay walked into the office and saw his entire team using an internal voice-to-text tool instead of the hardware they had spent years building. That was the moment he realised the real opportunity was the software.

5. He made the hardest call of his career

Overnight, they scrapped the Bluetooth earpiece entirely. Rebuilt Wispr Flow as a software product from scratch and launched it to the public in 8 weeks.

6. Silicon Valley couldn't stop talking about it

2 to 10,000 users in 30 days - purely word of mouth. VCs were already using it. That led to raising $81 million and a post-money valuation of $700M.

7. Then Tanay came home

India was always the next chapter. Hinglish support, Android launch, ₹320/month pricing. 

Their long-term goal is eventually bringing it down to ₹10-20/month - because Tanay wants voice AI to reach far beyond just premium users.

Today, India is Wispr’s second-largest market after the US. And the company says it retains nearly 70% of users even after 12 months.

From getting shut down by Google to finally building the voice product he dreamed about as a kid, Tanay stayed obsessed with the same problem for nearly two decades.

And honestly, it was the best thing I ever figured out about myself.

For years, I was doing what I thought a founder was supposed to do. Weekly reports. Team check-ins. Tracking deadlines. Managing people. I was doing all of it, but I was never enjoying any of it.

I was burning out and couldn't figure out why. So I reached out to my mentor Ronit. He sat me down and told me:

"You're someone who builds things from scratch. Stability and managing people drains you because that's not who you are.”

I sat with that for a while. And the more I thought about it, the more I realised he was right.

Because when I'm honest with myself, I love starting things. Building from zero. Experimenting with new ideas and directions. That's where I come alive.

But managing people day to day, chasing reports, resolving conflicts - that was never me. And the more I forced myself into that role, the further I got from the work I actually loved.

That's when it hit me that this was never a workload problem. It was a self-awareness problem.

I really wish someone had pulled me aside on day one and asked - what kind of founder are you, really? Not what kind do you think you should be.

So I'm asking you that now.

Are you a builder or an operator?

content:
My parents sold their car and jewellery to pay my college fees.
4 years later, I handed my dad the keys to his dream bike.

In my final year of engineering, my dad called.

“Beta, we can’t afford this year’s fees.”

The car was already gone. My mom’s jewellery too. Years of savings, gone.

And still, it wasn’t enough.

I remember hanging up and just sitting there. One thought kept repeating: I cannot let this be for nothing.

The next few years were hard. Failed ideas. Borrowed money. Constant uncertainty. 

My dad never complained. But I noticed things. Like how he'd slow down every time we passed a Royal Enfield showroom. Same bike. Same look in his eyes. He never asked for it. I never forgot.

And eventually, one business succeeded. NB Media.

Once it became stable, I kept thinking about that bike. The one he always stopped to look at.

One day I thought - if I can afford it now, what am I waiting for?

4 years after that phone call, I rode it home and called him outside.

He didn’t rush.
He walked slowly.
Ran his hand over the bike, like he was making sure it was real.

I handed him the keys and he hugged me. And I had to walk away so he wouldn't see me cry lol.

I’ve had wins in business I’m proud of. But nothing has ever felt like that moment. Not even close.

And that's the only thing that has ever really mattered.

#parents #success #growth

In 2023, Jesse met Ashwin Sreenivas at a founders retreat - a fellow founder with a previous exit. When GPT-4 launched, instead of rushing to build, they spent weeks talking to potential customers, just to find a problem worth solving.

4. One expensive problem kept coming up

Enterprises were spending millions on support teams answering repetitive queries. Everyone wanted one thing: automation. Investors said it was crowded. Jesse ignored them.

5. So they built Decagon

Decagon automates customer support across chat, email, voice, and SMS. But the real difference was that it actually worked for complex queries. Not just basic chatbot replies.

6. They built exactly around that pain

For the first 6 months, they didn’t guess features. They built directly from customer requests. Early users like Vanta shaped the product. Which meant faster results, and nothing got wasted.

7. That’s what created pull

They hit 7-figure ARR in 6 months. So instead of chasing investors, investors came to them with pre-emptive offers. Only then did they raise at a $1.5B valuation.

Most founders chase investors. Jesse made investors chase him. And the only thing that made that possible was being too busy building something people actually wanted.

The reason you don't have a successful business idea yet - is because you're looking for the “perfect” one.

Brian Chesky didn't "think up" Airbnb. He was broke, couldn't pay rent, and had a spare air mattress. So he rented it out to strangers.

I experienced the same thing. I tried becoming a YouTuber and failed. But I learnt video editing in the process. 

Started freelancing at ₹500 per video because I needed money. Then realised dozens of creators had the same problem - they needed videos but couldn't afford agencies.

I wasn't looking for a business idea. I was trying to survive. NB Media came from that.

Most people do it the other way around. They sit with a notebook, brainstorm 50 ideas, and wait for the right one to hit. And they stay stuck.

Here's a better way to find your idea. Answer these:

1. What pisses you off every week? Something you keep running into, over and over again.

2. What have you figured out for yourself? A shortcut, a system, a fix. If you built it for yourself, others need it too.

3. What do people keep asking you for? If the same request keeps coming up, you're already solving a problem.

Once something clicks - that's when you test it. Talk to people, ask if they'd pay for it, and find out fast if it's worth building.

Stop waiting for the lightbulb moment. Start paying attention to what's already broken around you.

Where did your best idea actually come from?

AI automation experts are selling workflows worth ₹20 lakh for ₹20,000.

The market is hot. Companies are desperate for their service. But most of them are still broke.

And the problem isn't their skill. It's how they price.

Here's the AI Cost Justification Framework that fixes that:

Step 1: Map the process you're automating

Who does it, how long does it take, and how often?

Example: A company has 3 support agents, each spending 6 hours a day on tickets, at ₹400/hour.

Step 2: Calculate what it's costing them annually

6 hours x ₹400 x 3 agents x 300 working days = ₹21.6 lakh/year on one process.

Step 3: Estimate your impact

Your workflow automates 80% of those tickets. That's ₹17.2 lakh saved every year.

Step 4: Price at 20% of annual savings

That's ₹2.8 lakh for one project. The client still gets a 5x return on what they paid you. And you have a number you can defend.

Compare that to the freelancer who thinks:

“I built it in 10 hours, at ₹2000 per hour that’s ₹20,000.”

P.S - If you’re scared of competitors underpricing, find a niche and specialise.

content:
The startup culture I built almost destroyed my company.

For the first few years, I hired people like me - young, energetic, hungry.

We built this fun, laid-back vibe. No strict rules. No corporate BS. Everyone loved it. And we scaled to 90 people and hit 8 figures.

Then things started breaking.

Projects stalled unless I personally pushed them. Deadlines became suggestions. People waited for me to decide everything instead of just doing it themselves.
Everyone was busy, but output stayed flat.

I thought we had a motivation problem. Or maybe I'd hired the wrong people.
But the real issue was the culture itself.

So here's what I'm doing differently now:

1. Stopped hiring only young people

I used to think energy mattered more than experience. I was wrong. Now I'm bringing in a senior Head of Production as our highest paid hire.

2. Broke the production team into capsules

One person managing 90 people doesn't work. So we split into 4-5 capsules of 9-10 people each. One manager per capsule. Clear ownership, no confusion.

3. Made deadlines non-negotiable

Before, missing a deadline was fine because "we're all learning together." Now? Deadlines are commitments. And if you miss them repeatedly, you're out.

4. Killed the "we're all founders here" vibe

Everyone had opinions on everything - but nobody wanted to own anything. We fixed that by making roles airtight. If it's in your function and it fails, it's on you.

I'm not saying the fun culture was a mistake. It got us here. But what got you to 90 people won't get you to 100.

Know when to evolve, or the culture you built will be the thing that breaks you.

What signal told you it was time to change yours?

content:
My parents sold their house, car, and jewellery to pay my college fees.

And it shaped how I saw money for years.

My father had a scarcity mindset - not by choice, but by circumstances. Every rupee was counted twice before being spent. Protecting what little we had became survival.

Growing up like that, I learned discipline the hard way. When I started NB Media, that scarcity thinking saved me:

-No wasteful spending.
-Lean operations.
-Every rupee had to justify itself.

But as we started growing, I noticed the same mindset that helped me survive was now stopping me from scaling.

I was afraid to invest in better tools.
Hesitant to hire the right people due to budget constraints.
Refusing to spend on growth because "what if we lose it all?"

That's when I realised - my parents practiced scarcity because they had to. I was practicing it out of fear. And that needed to change.

So I started practicing abundance in business:

-Hired people smarter than me.

-Invested in a car, a house and health too.

-Spent on international trips and experiences.

-Spent on systems that freed up 200+ hours every month.

We scaled to 8 figures. And the first thing I did after I got some money was I bought back everything my parents lost.

My father's dream bike now sits in our garage.
My mother wears the gold she once sold (and more!)

They don't count every rupee anymore - because they don't have to.

That phone call from my final year - "Beta, we can't afford the fees" - still haunts me. 

But now, when I see my parents living without financial worry, I know their sacrifice wasn't for nothing.

What's one money lesson your parents taught you that you still follow?

content:
Tanmay Bhat built a team with a 100% retention rate.

Which is rare because in the creator economy, people leave all the time.
Editors get better offers. Writers go freelance. People jump for small hikes.

So how did he keep the same team together for 4+ years?

He had one simple rule - whenever a brand deal comes in, everyone who worked on it gets a cut:

-Editors
-Cast members
-The whole team

So when a brand deal lands, it's not just good news for Tanmay. It's good news for everyone.

Here, the better the video performs, the more you make.

So people don’t just show up to work. They root for the video to do well. They want the brand deal to land.

That’s a completely different feeling from a normal workplace. And that feeling is what keeps people from ever wanting to leave.

Most teams are built the opposite way. You get paid for your role, the company keeps the upside. So the moment someone offers you 30% more, you take it. There's no reason not to.

Tanmay removed that temptation entirely.

Now people don't just execute tasks. They care about performance. They stay longer. 

And most importantly they stop comparing salaries and start comparing earning potential.

Retention isn't a culture problem. It's an incentive problem.

If your team only benefits from stability, they'll leave for a better offer.
If they benefit from growth, they'll stay to build it.

content:
Nobody talks about how intimidating going to networking events is, especially alone.

I just took a solo trip to Dubai for a creator event and it nearly broke me.

I'm an extrovert by nature. The loudest person at every party. 

But as soon as I entered this event, I started panicking. Everyone seemed to know each other. I had no one to fall back on. I felt completely out of place.

For the first 30 minutes, I just stood there watching people walk up to strangers and start talking.

Then I suddenly saw RJ Abhinav across the room. I'd been following his work for a while. And I had this split-second thought - if I don't walk up to him now, I never will.

So I just did it. Walked over. Introduced myself. It was awkward at first. But we ended up talking for a bit.

That one conversation broke the ice for me.

After that, I kept going. Started more conversations. Sat next to random people between sessions. Pushed through the discomfort.

By the end of the trip, I'd had lunch with RJ, got invited to parties, and met creators I'd been following for years.

Here's what I realised about solo networking: The discomfort lasts only for an hour. People who show up alone are easier to connect with. They're intentional. They remember you.

If you've been skipping events because you don't have someone to go with - just go.

The first conversation will be terrifying. But everything after that gets easier and you’ll end up making great connections.

When's the last time you showed up somewhere alone?

#networking #entrepreneurship

Aarohi & Avni started posting 2 months ago, and they already have 700K+ followers. But they don’t even exist.

I know the creator who built this account, and he told me exactly how he did it.

Here's the process he followed:

1. Built the character's prompt using ChatGPT

He used a Custom ChatGPT with an AI UGC plugin. Fed in a reference image - pose, outfit, background. It wrote the full prompt covering skin texture, lighting and setting. 

When something looked off, he asked for the prompt in JSON format, edited just that one element and regenerated instead of starting from scratch.

2. Generated the image in Google Flow

He fed that prompt into Google Flow with the character's face as a reference. He looked for two things - natural skin texture and slight imperfections. He kept regenerating until it felt like an actual person.

3. Generated the video and voice after that

For movements, he used Higgsfield motion control. For speaking and ad content, he used Veo inside Google Flow. He gave them a dialogue and Veo generated a consistent voice automatically - no separate voice cloning tool needed. 

After 2 months of Build → Test → Post → Repeat:

-700K+ followers
-100M+ views from a single video
-Closed brand deals worth ₹5L+
-Celebrity DMs “OMG they look so real”

AI influencers are already here, already growing, already making money. And the window to get in early is still open.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 month ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
If you want to be an entrepreneur, reject every corporate job offer you get.

No matter how good it pays.

I know how this sounds. Reckless. Naive. Privileged.

But I rejected offers from Cognizant and Infosys. My friends thought I was crazy. They took the corporate jobs. Better pay, better perks, better "career trajectory."

I spent two years not knowing what I was doing. Failing at everything I tried. No income, no direction, just frustration.

But here's what I figured out:

1. Corporate comfort is the biggest enemy of entrepreneurship

That ₹15 lakh salary is a trap. You get comfortable. You start planning around that paycheck. You tell yourself "just one more year" until the raise, the promotion, the bonus. Five years later, you're still saying the same thing.

2. The "training" they promise? It's a lie

Big companies will tell you you're learning. Leadership. Management. Strategy. What you're actually learning is how to work within systems someone else built. And entrepreneurship is about building the system yourself. 

3. Time is the real cost - not the money you're turning down

Starting a business at 22 with no experience is terrifying. Starting at 30, with a salary you depend on, a family to support, and EMIs to pay? Nearly impossible. The longer you wait, the more reasons you create not to start.

Those two years of struggling taught me more than any corporate job ever could. I learnt to build. To sell. To fail, and keep going.

Some of my friends are still waiting for the "right time" to leave.

Meanwhile, today at NB Media - we have a team of almost 100 people, doing 8-figure INR revenue.

I'm not saying everyone should do this.

But if you're not sure about entrepreneurship, take the job. There's no shame in that. But if you KNOW you want to build something - don't waste years preparing.

There's no right moment. There's only the moment you decide to start.

Did you take the corporate job, or did you bet on yourself?

#entrepreneurship #career #corporate

content:
I failed 7 times before NB Media. And each one taught me exactly what I needed to build it.

My first attempt was in 8th grade. Then through college, I kept trying - different ideas, different approaches. All of them failed.

But I don't regret any of it.

Here are the businesses that I tried, and what they taught me.

▷ 1. Sold CCTV cameras in 8th grade

Cold-called local businesses for commission. Made ₹6,000 and felt unstoppable. First time I realised - anyone can start. →You just need to pick up the phone.

▷ 2. Ran a laundry service in college

Missing dinner every night because of hostel laundry queues. Started collecting clothes door-to-door for cheaper. Never scaled. → The best businesses solve problems that personally piss you off.

▷ 3. Built Printo - a printout delivery service

Same frustration, different problem. Built a website, let students upload docs, delivered prints to their door. → You don't need a new idea. Just add convenience to what already exists.

▷ 4. Ran a Google My Business agency

Helped local businesses in Bathinda show up online. Made some money, shut it down in 2 months. → But proved that one skill, one solution, is enough to start.

▷ 5. Started a second-hand book community

Bookstores offered ₹50 for books I'd paid ₹500 for. Built a platform where seniors sold directly to juniors. → Bring the right people together, and value creates itself.

▷ 6. Launched Mojobites - a cake delivery service

No good bakeries nearby, so I delivered from the ones that were. Built the website in an hour. Made money the same week. → A simple idea, executed fast, is all you need.

▷ 7. Tried to become a YouTuber

Failed completely. But learned video editing, started freelancing at ₹500 per video. That became the foundation for everything that came after.

If you’re struggling to build your business and feeling overwhelmed, remember that failure’s just an expensive form of education.

And it’s all worth it. Because these lessons help you build something much bigger in the future.

What’s one lesson your startup journey has taught you?

#entrepreneurship #lessons #founders

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I traded 3 years of my 20s for an 8-figure business. I'd make that trade again. But not the way I did it. 

I spent 3 years grinding 16-hour days. Sleeping on the office floor. Surviving on Maggi. No friends. No fun. No life outside work.

I became "CEO" 24/7 and forgot the person I actually was.

I measured success by one thing: money in my bank account.

And NB Media hit 8 figures. We scaled to 90+ people. By every metric, we crushed it.

But by the end of 2024, I was completely burnt out.

I didn't want to come to the office anymore. I'd lost myself in the process. That's when I realised - the business was worth it but not at the cost of my personal life.

So here's what I'd do differently if I could go back:

-Set boundaries early. Work hours, personal time, non-negotiables. I thought boundaries were for people who weren't serious. I was wrong.

-Hire faster. I spent way too long trying to do everything myself. Bringing in the right people earlier would've saved me years of stress.

-Delegate sooner. I held onto tasks I should've handed off. Leadership isn't about doing everything - it's about building a team that can.

-Protect my identity. I became "CEO" and stopped being the fun guy at parties. Success doesn't mean losing who you are.

-Optimise for time, not just money. Revenue isn't the only metric that matters. Time with friends, health, experiences - those count too.

Looking back, I realise I thought suffering was part of the deal.

It's not.

You can build an 8-figure business without burning out. The grind is necessary. The suffering isn't.

What are you sacrificing right now that you don't actually need to?

#entreprneurship #founders #growth

content:
A $6.6 billion AI company's CEO just revealed how to make $10,000/month.
And you don't even need to code.

His name is Mati Staniszewski, co-founder of ElevenLabs.

While everyone is busy chasing chatbots and content AI, he pointed to an opportunity most people are completely ignoring - deploying AI voice agents for local businesses.

Here's the playbook that he shared:

1. Pick a niche

Local businesses lose thousands every month because nobody picks up the phone. Dental clinics, repair shops, restaurants, salons - they all depend on calls. Pick one and learn how their bookings work.

2. Set up the agent and build workflows

Feed it the business's knowledge - FAQs, pricing, opening hours. Then build simple logic around it. Booking an appointment? It checks the calendar. Pricing query? It explains. Ready to buy? It sends a checkout link on WhatsApp.

3. Connect the phone system

The AI simply answers the business’s existing number. It can integrate with tools like Twilio or their current phone system. From the customer's perspective, nothing changes - except now every call gets answered.

4. Pitch it the right way

You're not selling AI. You're solving a missed revenue problem. "Your nurses are answering calls between patients. Appointments are slipping through. I can fix that." That's a conversation they want to have.

5. Price it right and scale

Charge $500–$1,500/month per client. Get 7–10 clients in the same niche and you're at $10K/month. Build the workflow once, reuse it forever.

Now the business has a 24/7 AI receptionist.

Most small businesses still don't know this technology exists. The big AI companies are chasing enterprise clients.

Nobody is helping the millions of small businesses that still rely on phone calls.
That gap is your opportunity.

Which local business would you start with?

#ai #startups #founders

content:
Every "AI automation expert" I've met online is broke.
And I almost became one of them.

I spent ₹6 lakhs building an AI team at NB Media. It showed me why most AI automation businesses fail - and what actually works.

So here's the playbook you can use if you're starting today:

1. Stop asking AI to “generate business ideas”

Go manual - search LinkedIn for people venting about repetitive work, or check Upwork for tasks hired repeatedly. 

Upload as much of this data into ChatGPT as you can, then ask: "What patterns do you see?"

2. Turn those patterns into a real offer

One industry, one recurring problem. That’s all you need. See 20 Upwork listings for real estate lead qualifying? Boom, that’s your niche. Notice 40 agency owners complaining about hiring? That’s gold too!

3. Turn that problem into a real offer

A pattern becomes an offer when businesses have the same problem, it costs real hours weekly, and they're already paying someone to fix it. "Cut proposal drafting from 4 hours to 30 minutes" - that's an offer.

4. Build the workflow that solves it

Don't build a platform. Build one automated workflow. If HR managers spend 4 hours scanning 100 applications, create a Make/Zapier flow that pulls data, runs it through GPT, and shortlists in minutes.

5. Validate before you scale

Package it as a done-for-you setup. The more hours you're saving them, the higher you can charge. Reach out to 20 businesses. Show them exactly what their process looks like before and after your workflow. If 3-5 pay, you have product-market fit.

6. Your first client is also your biggest client

The person who paid, implemented it, and saw results? That's your custom automation client. Stop chasing strangers. Your best buyers already trusted you and got value. Double down there.

The market isn't saturated. Businesses need automation desperately.

But they don't need "AI experts" - they need someone who understands their problem and can solve it.

What industry do you understand well enough to spot the real bottlenecks?

#ai #automation #business

content:
Only 10% of my team wants to be a manager. And I think that's a good thing.

We sent a form to our entire team last year.
The question: "Who wants to step up as a manager next year?"

Out of 80 people, only 8 filled it out.
10%.

Most founders would panic at that number. Where's the ambition? Where's the drive?

But I'm relieved. 

Because the 10% who raised their hand actually WANT to lead. They're self-selecting.

If half the team wanted to manage, most would be doing it for the wrong reasons. More money. A better title. Status.

Your best executors would become mediocre managers.

And the truth is that not everyone should manage. 

Being great at execution doesn't mean you should lead people. Management is a different skill - holding people accountable, making unpopular decisions, developing others instead of just doing the work yourself. 

Most people don't want that. And that's okay.

So this year, we're building career paths where individual contributors can grow and earn more without managing anyone.

The 10% who raised their hands? Those are your future managers. The 90% who didn't? Let them be excellent at what they do.

What % of your team actually wants to manage?|

#management #growth #leadership

AI companies are making you dependent on their tools on purpose. 
And when they 10x the price, you won't be able to say no.

That $200 subscription wasn't priced that way because it costs $200 to run. It was priced to get you hooked.

And when it becomes $2,000 - because it will - you won't have a choice. By then, your entire business will be built on it.

The difference with AI is the dependency is being built faster than anything we've seen before.

The internet bubble took almost a decade to burst. AI won't take that long because companies are integrating these tools into their core operations in months, not years.

And here's what will happen when the pricing spike comes:

They'll lose their ability to function. Because they didn't just outsource their execution. They outsourced their thinking. 

That's the part nobody is talking about.

The people who will survive a pricing spike will be the ones whose work doesn't stop when a tool disappears. 

The ones who spread across multiple tools, upskilled their teams, and understood the principles well enough to adapt.

So use AI tools as aggressively as you want. Just make sure your business can still survive without them.

If your most used AI tool doubled its price tomorrow, what would you do?

I've had 7 business ideas crash before NB Media hit 8 figures.

Each one felt like a huge waste of time and money. But that’s what I needed to figure out what actually works.

Here's what most founders don't realise:

You're only doing what you already know will work. And that means you're leaving the biggest opportunities on the table.

I learned this from founders running billion-dollar companies. They aim for at least a 30% failure rate.

They take safe bets to keep the business running. And then take wild bets to 10x it. 

Amjad Masad got rejected by Y Combinator four times before Replit became a $3 billion company. 

Travis Kalanick had two companies fail before Uber. Scour went bankrupt. Red Swoosh barely made it. Then Uber became worth over $100 billion.

One breakthrough makes up for ten failures.

For me, those 7 failed businesses were the wild bets that didn't work.

But they taught me what customers don't actually want, what ideas sound good but fall apart in execution, and what doesn't work.

And now NB Media only exists because I was willing to fail 7 times first.

1 out of 8, that’s a 87.5% failure rate. And it’s all worth it.

How many projects did you fail at last year?

content:
You can start a solo AI business in 2026 - without knowing a single line of code.

Most people think they need to be a programmer to build an AI business. That's the biggest lie holding them back.

I built an AI team for my agency without writing a single line of code. It cost me ₹6 lakhs and saves us 200+ hours every month.

Here's exactly how you can start a solo AI business with 0 technical background:

1. Find a painful problem in a market you understand

Call 10 people in a niche you understand. Ask about their biggest frustrations. Look for problems that help them make money, save time, or save money.

2. Solve it manually first

Create a one-page offer: problem, outcome, timeline, price. Charge them to do it manually. Get paid to learn the workflow. 

3. Build a clickable prototype

Use Figma, UXPilot, or Visally to create photo-realistic prototypes from prompts. Show it to early customers for feedback. You haven't spent a rupee yet.

4. Validate with cash before building

Launch an "early adopter program" with 10-50 early customers at 50% off annual pricing. Frame it as exclusive early access. 

5. Build your MVP with no-code AI tools

Go to BuildWithAI.io and explain your idea in plain English. It gives you system prompts. Copy those into Lovable.dev and watch it build your AI app in real-time.

6. Watch what customers do, not what they say

Set up weekly interviews, but check your logs to see what they actually use.
Prioritise what impacts paying customers most. 

7. Find distribution channels no one else is using

Partner with people who have your audience. Offer referral fees.Build integrations for Zapier, Notion, or Shopify. Get featured in their marketplace.

No coding required. Just clear communication.
This is how real AI businesses are being built in 2026.

The tools are cheap or free. The barrier isn't technical anymore.

It's whether you can spot a problem worth solving and actually take action on it.

What problem in your industry could you solve this way?

article.linkLabel: Open article: Head of Media Operations by ytmoney.keka.com

content:
I'm hiring for the highest paid position in my company, at 15 LPA.

And honestly, I should have done this 2 years ago.

When I started NB Media at 21, I built the team around "good vibes." Young, fun people who loved hanging out together. It felt like we were building something special.

And it worked at 20 people. Even at 30.

But now we're a team of 100, and I feel like I’m the only one holding things together. 

Whenever I take time off, performance gets affected. 

Some people get distracted and take things easy when I’m not around, because there’s no real leader in charge. 

So here's what I learned the hard way:

Experienced team members are more expensive for a reason. You can’t run a company full of freshers in their early 20s.

I have to stop trying to hire more people like me, and hire someone BETTER at bringing the best out of people.

So now, I’m looking for my Head of Operations.

Someone with 5 years of experience in media, who can:

-Build & implement systems that scale without compromising quality

-Take full ownership of operations across our production pipeline

-Take care of execution and accountability across 100+ people

I care less about degrees and qualifications, and more about your [skill+mind]set.

It’s not an easy role. It’s high stakes.

But it’s highly paid, with immense learning and growth prospects.

If you’re interested:

1. Comment ‘LEADER’ below
2. Send me a connection request

I’ll DM you the application link.

#hiring #operations #culture

content:
I joined my friend's startup as a co-founder and quit in the next 30 days.

Here's why walking away was the best decision I made.

A friend was building an AI content tool. The product was solid, the market was booming, and he wanted me on as co-founder.

I was fresh off a Europe trip, energised, and exploring new opportunities. So when he asked, I didn't hesitate. I said yes.

We moved fast. Invested ₹6 lakhs. Hired a team. Took office space. And for the first few weeks, it felt exciting. 

But slowly reality started hitting me.

I realised that I wasn't technical enough for the role I'd signed up for. And I didn't have the time or bandwidth to commit the way a co-founder should.

Every time I couldn't show up the way he expected me to, it felt like I was letting him down.

So I had two options:

1. Stick around for 6 months, half-ass both businesses, and probably lose a friend.

2. Or quit now, save the friendship, and get my focus back.

I chose the second and told him the truth.

"I'm excited about what you're building. But I'm not the right person for this. I don't have the time or the technical depth.”

I offered to cover all the expenses. No hard feelings. No drawn-out drama.
Just a clean exit.

The friendship survived. And I got my focus back on NB Media.

Here's what most people get wrong: They think quitting shows weakness. That going along no matter what proves commitment.

But the truth is, the faster you recognise misalignment, the less damage it does.

To your time. 

To your energy. 

To your relationships.

Have you ever had to walk away from an opportunity to protect your focus?

#startups #cofounders #entrepreneurship

content:
Someone asked me this question recently, and I was surprised how quickly I made the decision.

I realised skillset matters much less to me than I thought.

Most founders keep Person A and "manage" the trust issue.
I fire Person A without hesitation.

Because performance is trainable. Trust isn't.
I can teach someone to edit faster, pitch better, close more deals.

But I can't teach loyalty. I can't teach someone to have my back when things fall apart.

So Person B is irreplaceable.

At NB Media, our team members who might not have had the best skillset, but had our trust - became our best leaders.

The "rockstars" who didn't share our values didn't last long.

Skills can be taught in months. Trust takes years to build and seconds to break.

When choosing between short-term performance and long-term trust?

Choose trust. The performance will follow.

Do you agree, or am I being too idealistic here?

#employees #leadership #founders

content:
Building a video editing agency made me miserable.

Not because it was failing. NB Media scaled to 90+ people, and by every metric, we were crushing it.

But somewhere along that way, I stopped being myself.

I used to be the fun guy at every party. Energetic, spontaneous, always down for anything.

Then I became a CEO.

And as CEOs we are supposed to be serious, right? Focused. Always working. Always "on." So that's what I became.

I stopped hanging out with friends. Stopped doing things just for fun. Every conversation became about the business.

And last year, I was completely burned out.

I realised that CEO is just ONE role you play. You're also a brother, a friend, a partner, and a human being.

But I was giving 90% of myself to the CEO role and barely 10% to everything else. I quickly realised that's not sustainable. 

And it's definitely not the life I want.

So this year, I'm choosing myself over my title.

-I’m hiring a Head of Operations to manage everything at my office even while I’m away.

-I'm living in Rishikesh for the entire month of February. Working remotely, giving myself space to just be.

-I'm planning some international trips which will most likely be spontaneous.

-I'm prioritising friendships, fun, and experiences over being in the office 24/7.

Because success shouldn't come at the cost of losing who you are.

Your business will grow. But make sure you're growing as a person too - not just shrinking into a title.

Are you losing yourself in your role too? 

#entrepreneurship #founders #ceo

content:
I said yes to 7 different projects - and ended up failing at most of them.

In the last few months, I had a lot of exciting opportunities… 
So I did the stupidest thing a founder can do:

I said yes to all of them.

→ Starting an AI product with a friend
→ Expanding our Instagram content vertical
→ Planning a podcast with a creator in Spain
→ Helping with a weight-loss brand on the side
→ Scaling our channels to Spotify & Facebook
→ Investing lakhs in an in-house AI vertical
→ PLUS running NB Media as usual

Every project needed “just an hour.”
But those hours piled up.
Decisions slowed.
Teams waited.

My deep work went down to 0%.

And everything moved at 10% instead of 100%.

I realised saying yes feels productive, but it’s actually how you kill your focus.

So I started shutting projects down.

I stepped out of the AI partnership - even though the product was strong.

Paused the podcast.
Stopped helping external brands.
Removed anything that didn’t directly strengthen NB Media.

I even told my friend, “I’ll repay every expense - I’m really sorry, I can’t give this the time it deserves.”

Tough call, yes. But the right call.

Because focus isn’t about doing more. It’s about having the courage to do less - deliberately, unapologetically.

Now everything is moving faster, cleaner, and with more clarity than before.

NB Media is breaking through targets, and our AI team has built 3 tools already.

Couldn’t be happier with this decision.

Do you believe side projects help you grow, or just slow you down?

#founder #entrepreneurship #growth #learnings

content:
I promoted my best employee to manager, and regretted it almost immediately.

Here's what happened:

Raghav* was excellent at his work. Great contributor. Empathetic, caring, and always understood when the team had challenges. The kind of person everyone loved working with.

So when we needed a manager, promoting him felt like the obvious choice.

But within a few months, I noticed something off.

The team was missing basic KPIs. Deadlines were slipping. Accountability had quietly disappeared.

People were taking his kindness for granted.

My first instinct was to blame him. Maybe he wasn't cut out for leadership.

But then I realised that he wasn't failing because he was bad at management. He was failing because I handed him a title and expected leadership to just... happen.

But being a great individual contributor doesn't automatically make you a great leader.

Knowing how to do the work is not the same as knowing how to:

-Enforce standards without being harsh.
-Balance empathy with expectations.
-Make decisions people won't like.
-Hold people accountable.

So now we're fixing it.

Here’s how:

1. We're implementing leadership training for our entire upper management team. 

2. We're creating proper transition programs for when we promote individual contributors to managers.

Because I've learned that management is a skill. And like any other skill, it needs to be taught too.

Do you think I took the right call?

*Name changed for privacy.

#employee #leadership #management #team

Here's why I did it:

A lot of founders think their team will naturally upskill and learn AI. But I don’t think that’s the best way.

If you want to build serious AI workflows and tools, you can’t rely on YouTube tutorials or random prompts. You need real technical understanding.

That’s why I enrolled myself - and my AI team - into a 6-month cohort to learn AI fundamentals. Not to become “AI influencers.” To build real internal systems.

And we've already built 4 AI tools internally at NB Media:

-A competition analysis tool that tracks trends across YouTube, Instagram, and LinkedIn.

-A title and thumbnail generator trained on our high-performing videos.

-A QA automation tool that catches errors before they reach our team.

-A topic filter that evaluates research before it hits our managers.

These tools save us 200+ hours every month.

My goal isn’t to reduce headcount. It’s to remove the grunt work so our team can actually think, create, and lead.

The ROI isn't immediate. But in 6 months, my team will be using AI to work more efficiently than ever.

Impact for each team member will go higher, allowing us all to do higher value work.

Before this, my team was wasting hours reviewing every single video before it went live.

-Trimming the boring parts that killed retention.

-Checking if the thumbnail was click-worthy.

-Picking the right background music.

-Fixing inconsistent audio.

More videos = more review time = bottleneck.

So we built something to fix it: An AI Video Checklister.

Here's how it works:

You upload your video. The AI does the rest.

→ Finds irrelevant clips and flags them
→ Trims dead spaces automatically
→ Reviews your thumbnail for engagement
→ Adjusts background music levels

This freed up my team to focus on what actually matters: strategy, ideation, and execution.

We publish more. We iterate faster. And we're no longer bottlenecked by manual reviews.

One of our clients hit 100K subscribers in 3 months using this system.

The tool didn't create better content. Our team did that.
But it removed the grunt work that was stopping us from doing our best work.

This is what I mean when I say AI-powered agencies are the future.

It's not about replacing humans. It's about freeing them from the chaos so they can focus on creativity.

content:
I'm paying ₹4L/month to an Instagram agency.
And honestly, it's the smartest investment I'm making this year.

Most founders would try to build this in-house. Hire a team, train them, set up systems.

But I'm running a 100-person agency and building AI systems. I don't have 6 months to waste setting up another vertical.

So I went with speed over control.

They made me an offer I couldn’t ignore: “100K followers in 6 months - or your money back.”

That's it. That's the offer. They don't ask me to "trust the process" or "give it time." 

And here's what I'm getting:
→ 20-25 posts/month
→ Scripts written in advance
→ I show up 2 days to shoot
→ They handle everything else

Two days of my time = 30 days of content.

The guarantee is what sold me. Because it flips the risk.

If they don't deliver, I get my money back. If they do, I get 100K followers and save 50+ hours a month.

That's a no-brainer.

And here's the framework any service business can steal:
[Exciting outcome] in [timeline] or [guarantee].

When you remove the risk, selling becomes 10x easier. People don't buy because they don't trust you yet. A strong guarantee fixes that instantly.

So if you're a service provider struggling to close deals, stop trying to "prove yourself" with case studies.

Just guarantee the outcome.

What outcome could you guarantee in your business?

#systems #startups #founders

content:
I don’t think I’d be an entrepreneur if it weren’t for my dad.

He didn't teach me business. He didn't give me money. He didn't even understand what I did in the beginning.

But he shaped the way I think about money - for better and worse.

Growing up, I watched him say “no” to almost everything that wasn’t essential.
Vacations. New clothes. Eating out.

Because in his world, every rupee had a purpose - survival.

At the time, I thought that mindset held me back.
But when I started my own business, I realised it also built me.

It taught me:

-How to stretch ₹10,000 like it’s ₹1 lakh.
-How to stay calm when money isn’t flowing in.
-How to make the most out of limited resources.
-And most importantly - how to respect what I earn.

But here's the thing - it also gave me a scarcity mindset that took years to unlearn. I was too afraid to spend on things that could actually grow the business. Too cautious to take calculated risks. Too focused on saving instead of investing.

I had to work on this. Hard. And I'm still working on it.

So no, my dad didn’t hand me a business. But he did hand me the discipline, humility, and patience to build one.

And that, I’ve learned, is the real inheritance most entrepreneurs overlook.

What's one trait you inherited from your parents that shaped your career?

#lessons #entrepreneurship #business

The small, constant tasks that require no real thinking - now handled quietly in the background.

When someone signs up for my newsletter, Make automatically:

→ waits three days and follows up with more useful resources

I built that workflow once - and it’s been running (almost) perfectly ever since.

2. Content creation on autopilot

I drop one idea into a spreadsheet.

Make triggers AI to:
→ Do thorough research
→ Generate the first draft
→ Build snippets for socials
→ Push everything to my scheduler

By Monday morning, I already have a week’s worth of posts ready for review. 

3. 24/7 client and customer support

An AI chatbot handles 90% of website queries. If a question is complex or from a high-value client, Make instantly creates a ticket, tags it as “priority”, and alerts me on Slack.

Now response times are 60% quicker, thanks to this automation.

All of this runs quietly in the background while I focus on building strategy, leading the team, and doing what actually moves the business forward.

Because when you automate the repetitive, you make more space for what really matters - creativity, freedom, and living your life.

content:
This tiny AI startup just beat Google's Gemini at half the cost.

It's called Poetiq. 

And their model topped ARC-AGI-2, one of the hardest benchmarks we have for measuring abstract reasoning in AI - basically, how close systems are getting to human-level thinking.

Google's Gemini 3 Deep Think recently hit 45%.
Their score? 54%.

You’d expect this to come from a massive new model, trained with billions in funding. That’s not what happened.

They did something way smarter:

1. They made existing AI models work together

Instead of training one giant model, Poetiq built a system that uses Gemini 3 Pro and GPT-5.1 together. Each model handles what it's already good at.

2. They made it think like humans do

Their system:

-Tries multiple approaches
-Tests ideas by running code
-Checks if results make sense
-Learns from what didn't work and tries again

The efficiency is mind-blowing.

Cost per task: $30 (vs Google's $77)
Accuracy: 54% (vs Google's 45%)
Team size: Only 6 people

They topped the leaderboard while cutting costs by 50%. And it was built in just 170 days.

Poetiq proved you don't need Google's budget to compete with Google. You need smart systems that multiply your output, not your expenses.

While tech giants burn billions training bigger models, a 6-person team showed the future belongs to those who orchestrate, not those who just scale.

Do you think this approach of combining existing AI models is the future?

content:
MrBeast calls it "scary times" for creators. And honestly… he might be right.
But not for the reason you think.

Last summer, MrBeast launched an AI thumbnail generator in his analytics tool. It was supposed to help creators make better thumbnails faster.

His fans destroyed him for it.

Within days, he had to pull it down and replace it with links to human artists instead.

Now, barely a year later, he’s warning that AI video tools like OpenAI’s Sora 2 could threaten how creators earn a living.

The irony? The point of it all.

He tried to profit from AI and got backlash. Now he’s scared of AI taking over.

He realised that the second his audience can't tell if he made the content, they stop caring. And that's the real threat.

MrBeast doesn’t make $85M a year because of production quality. He makes it because people trust him. They watch because it’s MrBeast doing insane challenges - not a faceless team.

AI threatens to kill that trust instantly.

If anyone can generate professional-looking videos with a prompt… why would audiences care who made it?

The creator economy was built on parasocial relationships and authenticity. AI doesn't just make content cheaper to produce - it makes the creator irrelevant.

And that's why MrBeast is scared.

Here's what this means for the rest of us:

AI won’t kill all creators. It will expose which ones were replaceable all along.

Those who built an audience on shortcuts, trends, or templates? They’ll fade.
Those who built it on perspective, stories, and trust? They’ll keep growing.

MrBeast isn’t scared of AI. He’s scared of losing the one thing no one can copy: the audience believing in him.

What do you think makes a creator irreplaceable?

#ai #mrbeast #creators

content:
Peter Thiel built PayPal into a monopoly using this simple 3-step framework.
And it’s more relevant than ever for AI.

Most AI founders build broad AI tools, spend heavily trying to match bigger players, and shut down within a year. 

But the framework he used is a direct solution to this:

1. Start small and own it completely

Pick one painful problem for one tiny group. Example: An AI assistant that automates GST filing workflows for CA firms, handling reconciliation, checks, and return prep - with the CA making the final submission.

The smaller your market, the easier it is to dominate 100% of it.

2. Scale strategically into adjacent markets

Once you dominate your niche, expand to related problems for the same customers. Started with AI for GST filing? Add income tax, audits, TDS compliance - problems your CA customers already face.

Don't jump to random markets. Grow sideways into what your existing customers already need.

3. Create new value, don't compete directly

OpenAI builds general-purpose models for everyone.

Your opportunity is to build vertical AI on top of those models - pre-load Indian tax rules, add Tally integration, enable ITR auto-fill - so professionals don't have to train ChatGPT from scratch every time.

Don't worry about being first. Worry about being so good that no one bothers switching.

Start small. Dominate completely. Then scale into what your customers already need.

Which small niche do you think you could dominate first with AI?

#ai #startups #founders

I’ve tried balance.

Weekends off.
Fixed work hours.

So yeah. Some people build through consistency.
I build through going all in - followed by reflection.

And as long as it keeps pushing me (and my company) forward,
I’m okay being a little extreme.

If we miss even one post, anyone who DMs us that day gets ₹5000. No excuses. No exceptions.

Most people because the cost of quitting is zero.

When there's no real consequence for stopping, it's easy to skip a day. Then a week. Then you're done.

With money on the line, excuses vanish. You make time you thought you didn’t have. You show up even when you don’t feel like it.

content:
Cadbury's parent company just put $40 million into AI to cut marketing production costs by half.

This has scared creative agencies, but I think we're seeing this wrong.

Mondelēz International - the company behind Cadbury and Oreo - built a platform with Publicis and Accenture that automates how marketing content gets produced.

Videos, images, copy variations - all generated in seconds instead of weeks.

By next year, it'll be cranking out short TV ads. Maybe even Super Bowl spots by 2027.

But they're not the first company to build a tool like this.

Last year, Coca-Cola tried the same thing. They used AI to remake their iconic holiday ad - the one with the red trucks and snowy scenes.
The internet destroyed them.

People called it "soulless" and "creepy." Artists were furious. The AI-generated humans looked fake and lifeless.

Coke wanted to look innovative. They looked lazy instead.

So why is Mondelez going all-in after watching Coke crash?
Because they learnt something from this failure:

Don't automate creativity. Automate production.

Coke used AI to replace the magic - the emotion, the storytelling, the soul of the ad.

Mondelez is keeping their creative people for strategy, art direction - and using AI to replace the boring stuff - resizing assets, reformatting exports, creating platform variations.

The work that was never creative to begin with.

And they've built guardrails: no manipulative language, no unhealthy portrayals, no stereotypes. Humans review everything before it goes live.

So what I think is that Marketing is splitting into two lanes.

Lane 1: 'Productive' work - cheap, fast, AI automated.
Lane 2: Creative work - strategy, emotion, insight.

Most agencies have been charging creative rates for production work. AI just called their bluff.

Now, don't get me wrong - I'm all in on AI.

At NB Media, we built an AI team that saves us 200+ hours monthly.
But we learned early: AI works when humans control what matters.

Are you automating the right things?

#ai #cadbury #marketing #content

content:
I earned my first money when I was 13 - selling CCTV cameras for my dad’s shop in Bathinda.

He gave me a simple deal: “I won't give you any salary, but you’ll get 20% commission on every sale.”

But I didn’t know sales. Didn’t know what a pitch was. Didn’t even know how to handle rejection.

So I did what any 13-year-old would do - I Googled.
Found phone numbers of nearby companies. Started cold calling them one by one.

Most people laughed.
“Beta padhai pe dhyaan do.”
“Does your dad even know you’re doing this?”

After two months of NOs, one person finally said yes.
That “yes” earned me ₹6,000.

My first ever earned money. I was over the moon.
But the money wasn't what changed my life - it was what that experience taught me.

That first sales experience at 13 taught me lessons I still use today:

1. Commission rewires how you see money

Most people grow up thinking: work 8 hours = get paid for 8 hours. But commissions showed me you can make ₹6,000 in one conversation if you solve the right problem for someone.

2. The real skill was figuring things out

Googling scripts, handling rejections, learning on the fly - that’s what business really is. Acting with incomplete info and adjusting as you go.

3. The first “yes” changes everything

The money was nice, but the “yes” was the real win. One sale means the system works - that a stranger will actually pay for what you built. That flips your perspective permanently.

4. Rejection at 13 kills your fear of looking stupid

When grown adults laugh at you over the phone and you still show up the next day, embarrassment stops being a big deal. That changes how you handle almost everything later in life.

That ₹6,000 didn’t make me rich.

It made me obsessed - with building, learning, and creating things people value.

Looking back, that was the real start of my entrepreneurial life - not when I started an agency, but when I realised I could build my own opportunities from scratch.

When did you earn your first money?

#entrepreneurship #earnings #success #growth

content:
These four AI business ideas have killed over 200 startups.

In startup language, these are “tarpit ideas” - concepts that feel obviously valuable, attract smart founders and capital, but are structurally hard to turn into durable businesses.

They sound great on paper but have some hidden problems that end up bankrupting startups.

So here are 4 such AI tarpits you should avoid:

1. Thin GPT Wrappers

Jasper AI hit $1.5B valuation in 2022, then saw massive churn and growth collapse after ChatGPT launched at $20/month.

If your product is just GPT + UI, you're competing with the source which results in no moat, no pricing power and no business.

2. AI Scheduling Assistants

Scheduling automation only works when everyone trusts the bot and behaves predictably. Most people don’t - which is why simple tools like Calendly won.

3. AI Content Generators

AI content tools promised virality. But as more people used them, the posts started sounding the same.

When AI removes differentiation instead of amplifying it, engagement drops and users churn - unless it helps creators build taste, insight, or strategy.

4. AI Automation Agencies

Thousands of "we'll automate your business with AI" consultants flooded the market in 2023-24.

Most collapsed within months. The work was commoditised and delivering real automation required deep technical expertise these agencies didn't have.

Tarpit ideas aren’t just “bad ideas.” They’re deceptive ideas - they look easier than they are and trap founders into long, low-leverage paths.

Because the real challenge in AI isn’t just building technology. It’s choosing problems that can support durable differentiation and long-term value.

What other AI ideas do you think fall into the tarpit category?

#ai #tarpit #business #founders

The team missing basic KPIs. 

Decisions waiting for me.

Deadlines slipping.

1. No one knew where bottlenecks were because we had no dashboards tracking production flow.

2. Managers couldn't make calls because I'd never trained them to lead without me.

3. The team waited for my input on everything because that's what I'd conditioned them to do.

So here's what I’m doing now:

-Building dashboards that show what's stuck in real-time.
-Creating systems that catch problems before they explode.

If your business can't run without you, you're trapped.

content:
Raj Shamani built 10M+ followers using a simple 3-part content framework.

Here's the ECG system you can steal:

E - Evergreen Content

Content that stays relevant forever. Think "how-to" guides, frameworks, foundational knowledge. This builds long-term value. People discover it months or years later and still find it useful.

When to use it: 40% of your content should be evergreen. It compounds over time.

C - Controversial Content

Content that takes a strong stance. It challenges popular opinions and sparks debate. This drives engagement. People either love it or hate it, but they CAN'T ignore it.

When to use it: 30% of your content should be controversial. It gets people talking.

G - Growth Content

Content optimised purely for reach. Trending topics, viral formats, hooks designed to stop the scroll. This brings new eyeballs. It's your discovery engine.

When to use it: 30% of your content should be growth-focused. It expands your audience.

Most creators overdo ONE type:

Only evergreen → boring
Only controversial → exhausting
Only growth → shallow

Raj balances all three. And that’s the game.

I’ve seen creators fix just their content mix and double engagement in under a month - without posting more.

So which of these three are you using the least right now?

#creator #framework #content

Let me explain.

We had this insanely talented editor. He knew our entire workflow inside out. Clients loved his work. Projects moved fast when he was around.

Then one day, he quit. No notice. Just gone.

Our production pipeline collapsed for 2 weeks. Deadlines missed. Clients frustrated. The team scrambling to figure out how he did things.

That's when it hit me - I'd built my agency on a dangerous foundation.

Everything was on people. Nothing was documented. No systems. No SOPs.

If someone was sick, on leave, or quit - we were screwed. I knew I had to fix this, fast.

So I made a hard decision:

1. Stopped all new projects for a week

2. Sat with every team member to document their processes

3. Created SOPs for editing, client management, file handling

4. Built a central knowledge base everyone could have access to

It was painful. But it saved the business.

Today, NB Media runs smoothly even when people take leave or even resign.

We've scaled to 100+ people because no single person is a bottleneck anymore. Not even me.

And that’s the real test of scale.

A business shouldn’t depend on any one person to function, including the founder.

Do you think depending on employees too much can backfire?

Everyone's posting their revenue targets, client numbers, growth plans. I've done it too.

But this year taught me something: what you say NO to matters more than what you say yes to.

So here are my anti-goals for 2026:

1. I’m not firefighting anymore

I'm not jumping into every daily crisis anymore. Not being the bottleneck for every decision. This year, I realised my business only worked when I was there. 

So in 2026, I'm building systems - dashboards, trained managers, clear processes - so the business runs without me.

2. No opportunities outside my core business

This year, I started 8 projects and I quit 7 of them.

So now, I'm only saying yes to work that aligns with NB Media.

3. No work that's just for money

Time and energy are my real constraints now.

If a project drains me, doesn't align with where we're going, or requires me to be someone I'm not - it's a NO.

4. No sacrificing personal time

I built this business for freedom. But somewhere along the way, I created a dependency.

In 2026, I'm protecting my schedule. Taking time off without guilt. Doing things that actually make me happy.

Because what's the point of success if you can't enjoy it?

Your goals tell you what to chase.
Your anti-goals tell you what to protect.

Most founders only define the first. Then wonder why they're busy but not happy.

In 2026, I'm protecting my focus harder than I'm chasing growth.

What do you need to say NO to in 2026?

content:
OpusClip (the AI video clipping tool) went from 0 to $215 Million in 2.5 years.

Young Z. (the CEO) just broke down his exact 30-day playbook for building an AI startup in 2026.

Here's the roadmap you can steal to build your startup:

► Days 1-21: Stop building. Start obsessing over one problem

Do 20-30 customer interviews. Pick people from different industries, budgets, and geographies.

Your idea MUST pass these 3 tests:

→ Boring test - Is it solving a boring problem? 
If it sounds cool, it’s probably overcrowded.

→ Niche test - Can you segment it further? 
If yes, take the leap and do it.

→ Service test - Are people paying humans to do this manually? 
If not, there’s no real pain.

► Days 22-25: Fake it before you make it (seriously)

Young didn't build Opus Clip first. He manually edited videos with AI and emailed the results to potential customers.

If 60%+ respond positively, you have early product-market fit. Then build a Discord bot. You don’t need fancy UI yet.

► Days 26-28: Crack distribution or die trying

Figure out how you'll acquire customers BEFORE you build the full product. 

Before writing a single line of code, answer:
→ Where does my ideal customer spend time online?
→ What's my customer acquisition cost?
→ Can I reach them without burning money on ads?

► Days 29-30: The ChatGPT test

Can GPT-5 or Gemini do this in their next release? If yes, you're dead.

Your advantage will come from:
→ Proprietary data you collect as users grow
→ Deep workflow integration (not just a chatbot)
→ A specific niche where general AI doesn't work well enough

Opus Clip became a $215M company by following this exact process.

What's the biggest challenge you're facing while building your AI startup right now?

Meet Pranjali Awasthi.

While her friends were stressing about board exams, she was building Delv.AI - an AI research tool that academics couldn't stop using.

Her journey from coding with dad to raising millions at 18 is wild:

1. She saw researchers drowning in paperwork

During a neuroscience lab internship, Pranjali watched PhD students waste entire days scanning hundreds of research papers for one tiny insight.

That's when she realized - what if AI could read and summarize research papers instantly?

2. She deleted the complexity

Most tools try to make research "faster." Pranjali made it instant.

Upload PDFs → Ask questions → Get answers. That's it.

No dashboards. No copy-pasting. Just clean, usable insights in seconds.

3. She kept it lean and mean

10 people. Not 50.

Every task - from parsing documents to summarising findings - was handled by GPT-powered agents.

More users, same team size. That's how you scale smart.

4. She launched on her birthday (and let users build it with her)

Her MVP wasn't perfect. But it solved enough of the problem that real researchers started using it immediately.

From there, user feedback shaped every feature.

5. She built something researchers couldn't live without

When academics can't afford to stop using your product, investors notice.

Top VCs backed her. Delv AI reached thousands of researchers and secured significant funding.

Delv AI is still active today, helping academics worldwide save hundreds of hours on research.

But she didn't stop there.

At 18, she launched Dash - another AI tool that hit #1 on Product Hunt.

From learning to code at 7 to building AI tools the world actually needs by 18, Pranjali proved something important:

AI isn't here to replace teams. It's here to replace the boring stuff teams shouldn't be doing anyway.

While everyone's asking "Will AI take my job?" - Pranjali asked "What job should AI be doing instead?"

AI startups with MBA founders are destined for failure.

Harsh? Maybe. But it's the truth no one wants to admit.

Most non-technical founders think they can build AI companies like consulting firms - hire smart people, delegate the technical stuff, and focus on "strategy."

That's exactly why 90% of them fail before they even launch.

If you want an AI division in your current startup, hiring is the move.

But if you're building an AI company from scratch, your technical person needs to be your co-founder.

Here's how to find and convince the right technical co-founder:

1. Start with your existing network

The best engineers are people you've already worked with - classmates, colleagues, or internship buddies.

2. Don't chase unicorn CTOs

You need someone who can:
- Train models
- Build data pipelines
- Move fast with limited resources

3. Pitch the journey, not the idea

Engineers join adventures, not slide decks. Show them the challenge, the impact, and what they'll learn.

4. Prove you can execute without code

Get early customers, build partnerships, or create distribution channels. This shows your promise and convinces people to join.

5. Look for genuine commitment

Find someone who stays up late to fix problems because they care, not because they're paid to.

The truth is that 'upskilling' alone isn't enough to build a new company in AI. If you can't code and can't recruit a technical co-founder, you're not ready to build an AI startup yet.
