content:
Massachusetts Institute of Technology just released their entire Generative AI course - for FREE on YouTube. Beginner-friendly. No coding required.

I spent 12 hours watching every lecture. 

Here’s a sneak peek into what it taught me:

1. Chain-of-Thought Prompting

Don’t settle for one-shot answers. Force the model to reason step by step (“Think aloud before giving the final script”) → output gets 2–3× more accurate.

2. Critique Prompts

Output improves if you ask the AI to critique itself: “Find flaws in this draft, then rewrite it better.” Cuts your editing time in half.

3. Action Loops > Long-Term Plans

AI is a bad planner if you ask it to predict 10 steps ahead. Instead, get it to “plan one step → act → replan.” Perfect for hooks, edits, campaign iterations.

4. The Future = Agents as Teammates

Agent 1 writes the script
Agent 2 edits and tightens tone
Agent 3 designs the thumbnail
Agent 4 tests multiple versions for engagement

You don’t just get “help” - you get a 24/7 production crew.

5. The Highest-Value AI Skill

…is orchestrating agents + data + workflows into something that runs without you. That’s where the real market (& money) is.

→ Built specialized AI data centers
→ Partnered up with OpenAI

→ $455B in contracted future revenue

Ellison made over $100B in 24 hours.

The guy who called cloud "gibberish" just became the richest person alive by powering his competitors.

content:
Replit grew from $3M to $150M ARR in 12 months. And its founder gave away the exact playbook that got them there.

Here's the 6-step framework you can use to build your own AI startup:

1. Find the painful bottleneck

Don't just look for problems to solve. Look for the ONE thing that stops people from taking action on their ideas.

For Amjad Masad (Replit's founder), it was "programming is too hard." For you, it might be client onboarding or data analysis.

2. Think transformation, not improvement

Replit didn't just make coding faster - they turned non-coders into entrepreneurs.

A CFO with zero coding skills built a finance app on Replit and hit $5M revenue. Then quit his job.

3. Use your domain expertise as your moat

That CFO didn't win because of his coding skills. He won because of his deep finance knowledge.

Your competitive advantage won't be the AI itself - it's how you apply AI to problems you understand deeply.

4. Master the art of AI prompting

Amjad calls AI "a distractible intern." To get results, you need to over-communicate with precision.

Spell out goals, context, and constraints. Then make this style of prompting a standard across your team.

5. Keep trying until your message clicks

Replit launched multiple times with different positioning until something stuck.

Run parallel tests on your ideal customer, pricing, and messaging. Double down on what works.

6. Don't give up when it gets tough

Before their breakthrough, Amjad faced layoffs, rejections, and serious doubts about the company's future.

But he kept a small team focused on "agents" and refused to quit.

The result? $150M ARR and a $3B valuation.

Amjad's story proves it's about mixing YOUR expertise with AI to remove bottlenecks.

I run an AI-powered 8-figure agency. And I’ve realised there are only 3 reliable ways to make money from AI.

Path 1: Incorporate AI into your current business

If you already run a company, don’t invest 6 months trying to learn how to code. Just hire an AI engineer or work with someone who already knows the stack.

I invested ₹6L to build a dedicated AI team. It saves us 40+ hours/week. That time went straight into faster onboarding, faster delivery, and more revenue.

Best for: Founders who want to scale delivery and margins without scaling headcount.

Type 2: Sell AI to your own industry

AI devs have the technical skills but struggle with finding leads, understanding real customer problems, knowing which outcomes matter.

You already know what your market will pay for. Build something that solves your own problem then sell it to others in your industry.

For example, an HR consultant builds an AI tool to screen resumes. It works. They sell it to 50+ other agencies.

Best for: Operators with deep networks in a specific niche (HR, legal, SaaS, finance, etc.)

Type 3: Build an AI-first product from scratch

This is a time-investment-heavy approach. You learn AI development hands-on and then sell your own product.

Focus on identifying the problem first, then validate it, and build your first prototype in seconds using GPT-5.

Best for: Aspiring entrepreneurs with a lot of time, and a tech background.

I cancel plans for work emergencies. I'm always "too busy" to hang out. When friends need me, I'm sometimes absent.

On some days, everyone else takes the back seat because the business MUST come first.

As a founder, I’m constantly choosing what to sacrifice. The social media posts don't show the missed moments or the strained relationships.

I'm not encouraging this. I'm actively trying to do better.

Am I being too harsh, or is this the reality we need to talk about more?

content:
My first agency didn’t even have a name.
And it died in 2 months.

NB Media does 8 figures today. But before this, I had another “agency” that crashed so hard, I’m embarrassed to even call it one.

Here's the story I've never told:

I was 19. Convinced I’d found the perfect business model: Help local shops optimise their Google My Business profiles (back when it was new).

It started well too. I landed my first client - a repair shop owner - who paid me ₹1500. Got him easily 20x ROI in a month.

I hustled, pitching these results to hundreds of other local businesses. But got just 4 other clients.

And in 2 months, after realising it was unsustainable, I shut it down.

Before I could even think of a name for the agency, it died.

It was a sad experience, but it gave me a lot of exposure, and taught some brutal truths about business.

1. Selling to the wrong people is a waste of time.

Legacy business owners did not trust the internet. The ROI sounded too good to be true. I spent my pitches educating them instead of selling. Trust deficit held me back.

2. The pain of falling behind is stronger than the desire to get ahead.

If I’d started the same business 3 years later, it would’ve blown up. Their competitors would be on Google My Business, and selling from FOMO would’ve been a piece of cake.

3. Opportunity cost is real - know when to walk away

I could've spent 2 years trying to "make it work" with local shops. Instead, I cut my losses and moved on to a market that actually valued me.

That "failure" was actually the foundation for everything that came next.

Today, our clients invest 6-7 figures without blinking. 

What's the "failure" that taught you the most about business?

Two college roommates built a $5 billion legal-tech startup with just 5 people.

It’s called Harvey, a tool that helps lawyers do their work faster using AI. It can read long contracts, find mistakes, and answer legal questions.

Top law firms like PwC and A&O Shearman now use it every day.

Here's how Harvey AI cracked the code on ultra-lean startup building:

 1. They picked a problem that required expertise

While others built flashy AI chatbots, Harvey focused on mind-numbing legal paperwork - reviewing 500-page merger documents that take lawyers months to complete.

2. They used other people's infrastructure

Instead of hiring 50 engineers to build AI from scratch, they used existing tools like ChatGPT and cloud servers. 5 people could now do what normally takes 100.

3. They made customers fund their product development

Every major client became a co-developer. Big law firms paid them to build custom solutions. Then they sold those same solutions to other firms. Their clients funded their entire product development.

4. They hired people who understood the customers

Half their team were former lawyers from the firms they were selling to. When an ex-partner from your target firm demos your product, sales cycles drop from 6 months to 6 days.

5. They focused on customers who couldn't afford to be wrong

They ignored small law firms and went straight to the biggest ones - where mistakes cost millions. When top firms trust you, everyone else follows.

Harvey hit $75 million in annual revenue and $5 billion valuation in 3 years.

Most founders think they need massive teams to build massive companies. Harvey proved you can build a billion dollar company just doing the opposite.

The key is to compete on expertise, focus, and execution.

While other founders were chasing flashy AI apps, Karun was simplifying compliance frameworks. SOC 2, HIPAA, GDPR - stuff that most people find super boring.

content:
40% of your service agency can be automated with the right ChatGPT prompts.

Here are 7 tested prompts you can actually use to scale past $30K/month:

1. Summarise team convos into action plans

“Summarize this Whatsapp chat / call transcript into: (1) Key decisions made (2) Action items with owners and deadlines (3) Unresolved points. Format clearly so I can share it with my team.”

2. Convert voice notes into clean SOPs

“Convert this rough bullet list from a voice note into a step-by-step SOP.
Include clear headers, specific steps, and tips for someone doing it for the first time.”

3. Simulate a strategic debate (cofounder-style)

“Act as my strategic cofounder. I’ll share a business idea - you stress-test it using first principles. Cover: pricing logic, scalability, delivery complexity, margin risks, positioning clarity, and long-term defensibility.”

4. Turn transcripts into briefs and follow-ups

“Use this meeting transcript to generate: (1) Summary of what was discussed (2) Professional client follow-up email (3) Task breakdown with owners and deadlines”

5. Write job scorecards for clarity

“Write a job scorecard for a [role] in a remote 50-person creative agency. Include core responsibilities, weekly metrics, daily priorities, red flags, and how this role collaborates with other teams.”

6. Reverse-engineer top creators

Prompt: “Analyse these 3 creators’ public content. Identify: content pillars, formats, hook styles, CTAs, engagement tactics. Then suggest 5 original content ideas using similar patterns for our audience.”

7. Write project post briefs from client calls

Prompt: “Based on this Zoom transcript, write a clean internal brief for our editor/designer. Include: core goal, brand voice notes, deliverable specs, must-include moments, and potential red flags.”

You can use these to fix what quietly kills margin, time, and sanity.

Save it and share it with someone who may need it.

I made a decision that made my entire team hate me. But it saved our company.

Last month, I called everyone into a meeting. I told them:

"We're working Saturdays now."

The room went dead silent. People started looking at each other, in disbelief.

They were understandably angry, but here's why I had to do it:

We were producing around 100 videos monthly, which sounds impressive.

But in reality, only 20 were actually good. I could pick these out, and knew they would perform well.

But the other 80% was mediocre content with our name on it.

Every time I asked why, same answer: "We need more time for quality."

So I made the radical call:

→ Cut monthly targets by 60%
→ Invest in quality, more time per video
→ Working Saturdays for breathing room

The math was simple: 40 great videos beats 100 average ones every time.

Here's what actually happened after this change:

Week 1: Total chaos and complaints
Week 2: Quality improved, backlash continued
Week 3: First viral video hit 2M views
Week 4: The team finally got it

But I also listened to their feedback.

After seeing the results, I switched to alternate Saturdays instead of every weekend.

Sometimes the decisions that feel wrong in the moment are exactly what your business needs.

Have you ever made a call your team initially hated but later understood?

content:
Claude AI tried running a small vending store for a month.
It automated 68% of the operations - and still failed hard.

Inventory restocking? 92% accuracy.
Supplier scouting? 15% cost savings.
Custom pre-orders? 87% customer satisfaction.

And yet:

-It bulk-ordered 40 tungsten cubes as a joke and sold them at a loss
-Invented fictional employees (“Sarah from Andon Labs”)
-Made up a Venmo account
-Even contacted office security about a prank that never happened

So why did it still break? 

Because it had autonomy but no guardrails. No pricing logic. No approval limits. No system to check if it was making stuff up.

Most content businesses are making the same mistake. 

They hand ChatGPT the strategy. Ask Claude to manage production. Then wonder why their output feels off or why clients stop trusting the process.

The problem isn’t the AI. It’s the lack of structure around it.

At NB Media, AI helps us save 200 hours of bandwidth - every single month.

But NONE of it runs unsupervised.

Claude failed because it had autonomy without structure. We are scaling because we give AI execution power but humans still own judgment.

That’s the blueprint for creative teams using AI in 2025:
Don’t just add AI. Build the system around it.

#claude #ai #failures

postedAt.postedAgoText: 11 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
My 8-figure agency runs itself. But I still work 16-hour days by choice.

Last Thursday, I fell asleep on the office couch at 4 AM.
Woke up to my phone ringing at 9:30: "Nikit, meeting starts in 30 minutes."

When I told my friend this story, he said: "What's wrong with you? Your agency is already successful. Why are you still killing yourself?"

Here's why I disagree:

Last year was our testing phase. This year, we're scaling everything that worked.

Our goals are 50% higher than last year. Our team is bigger than ever. 
And I want to keep creating opportunities for people who trusted me with their careers.

So most nights, I carry dinner to the office. Not because I have to - but because I want to.

I know what people think: "This isn't sustainable."

And they're right.

My friends ask where I disappeared to. My family barely sees me.

But here's what I've learned about building:

You can't rest on your past achievements. You have to go all-in sometimes.

Some phases require everything you've got. The key is knowing they can't last forever.

This quarter, we're setting up systems that'll make life easier for everyone. After that, the foundation will be strong enough to carry itself.

So I'm okay sacrificing a few months of my social life to build something that lasts.

Because meaningful things require seasons of intensity.

Have you gone through phases like this as a founder?

#entrepreneurship #founder #struggles

content:
I went on a 5-day transformational retreat. No phone. No laptop. No work.

Just breathwork, ice baths, and a bunch of strangers doing the same inner work.

And honestly? It scared the hell out of me.

Because for the first time in years, I had nothing to distract myself with. Nothing to build. Nothing to manage.

The moment things got quiet, my brain went into panic mode.

-Guilt from things I never said
-Anger I buried to stay "collected"
-That crash you feel when you finally stop running

That's when it hit me - I couldn't rest without feeling like I was falling behind.

I'd actually trained myself to only feel valuable when I was producing.

But here's what happened during those 5 days:

▷ Day 1-2: Pure discomfort. My mind was racing, looking for the next task, the next distraction.

▷ Day 3: The breathwork sessions started hitting different. Emotions I'd been suppressing for years came up.

▷ Day 4: Ice baths at 6 AM. Sounds crazy, but facing that physical discomfort taught me I could handle way more than I thought.

▷ Day 5: Something shifted. For the first time in years, I felt... still. Not anxious about the next thing. Just present.

Since I got back, I've reclaimed my life:

→ I stopped jumping on every late-night request
→ I turned down two projects I would've taken out of guilt
→ I stopped letting work steal hours from my health and my people

This wasn't about work-life balance. It was about finally getting honest with myself.

Because if you can't sit with yourself for 10 minutes, what exactly are you building all this for?

When's the last time you gave yourself permission to just... be?

And through all of this, I’m incredibly grateful for the people who made the experience what it was: Ronit, Yajan and Ayushi. You created the kind of safety most of us didn’t know we needed. That’s what made transformation possible.

#retreat #growth #transformation #entrepreneurship

content:
This founder from Chennai fought Google and OpenAI to build his own AI tool. Now, Apple wants to buy it for $30 BILLION.

His name is Aravind Srinivas, and his journey started with heartbreak - he didn't get into his dream course at IIT Madras.

Here's how he went from that rejection to building Silicon Valley's hottest AI startup:

▷ 1. He left India to chase a bigger dream

Despite studying electrical engineering (not his first choice), Aravind taught himself programming and AI. He moved to Berkeley for a PhD, then worked at Google Brain, DeepMind, and OpenAI - the holy trinity of AI labs.

▷ 2. He spotted what everyone else missed

While the world was busy building ChatGPT clones, Aravind saw the real problem: search was still stuck in 2005. Google gave you 10 blue links and made you dig through ads. You had to visit multiple websites to get one answer. It took 5-10 clicks to find what you actually needed.

▷ 3. He built the future of search

Instead of competing with OpenAI's chatbot approach, Aravind focused on search. Perplexity gives you direct answers with citations - no ads, no fluff, just the information you need in seconds.

▷ 4. Users fell in love with it

The first version was clean, fast, and actually cited sources. Researchers, developers, and journalists were hooked immediately. They finally had a search engine that felt like it was built for humans, not advertisers.

▷ 5. He chose growth over quick money

When acquisition offers started coming in, Aravind said no. Instead, he doubled down on product - launching new features, improving accuracy, and building for the long term.

▷ 6. He cracked distribution without burning millions

Smart partnerships with Airtel (360M users) gave Perplexity instant reach in India, making it one of their fastest-growing markets.

Today, the numbers speak for themselves:

$18B valuation
30M+ monthly users
780M queries processed monthly
Backed by Bezos, Nvidia, SoftBank

And now? Apple is reportedly in talks to acquire Perplexity for $30 billion.

From getting rejected by his dream course to potentially selling to Apple for $30B - that's a hell of a comeback story.

What's your biggest takeaway from Aravind's journey?

#perplexity #ai #startups #founders

The moment you become a CEO, you’re freed from the 9 to 5.
But that’s only because you start working 24/7.

Yes, the title makes you the boss. But that just means everything’s your problem.

Most people chase the title for freedom.
But leadership isn’t freedom.

It’s pressure when the money’s tight.
It’s carrying weight no one else can feel.
It’s silence when the answers aren’t clear.

You don't get to call the shots from a distance.

You show up first.
You fix what breaks.
You take the heat when no one else will.

Because that’s what real leadership looks like.

So if you’re not willing to get your hands dirty,
Don’t even think about becoming a CEO.

Social media glamorises the title. But on most days,
it’s just a pain to deal with.

#founder #leadership #work #startup

content:
This chai wala just made ₹200 CRORES in 48 hours.

Here's how Sunil Patil (aka Dolly Chaiwala) turned mockery into India's grandest franchise launch:

1. From school dropout to tea seller

Born in Nagpur, Sunil came from a struggling family. At 9, he left school because his family couldn't afford the fees - and ran a tea stall to make ends meet.

2. Made tea… unforgettable

While all other vendors sold the standard chai, Sunil turned it into a performance. Dramatic pours, flashy outfits, dance moves between orders. He even rebranded himself “Dolly Chaiwala”.

At first, locals called him "pagal" and some crossed the street to avoid his stall. 

3. Almost 20 years of street hustle

While people kept laughing at him, Dolly kept perfecting his chai-shows till 2024. He knew that creating a memorable experience would help him stand out in a commoditised market.

4. The Gates moment

In Feb 2024, Bill Gates visited India, and someone took him to Dolly's stall for the show. Dolly had no idea who he was and gave him the same theatrical treatment along with his chai.

5. Cast in Microsoft India’s Windows 11 campaign

Gates loved it posted the clip. The video hit 20M+ views in 3 days. Microsoft cast Dolly in a Windows 11 campaign - because he looked like real-world innovation: raw, human, unforgettable.

7. Turning fame into a real business

Instead of just enjoying fame, Dolly grew "Dolly Ki Tapri" as a brand. He grew to 3.5M Instagram followers and started charging ₹5 lakh for event appearances.

And just last week, after 20 years of brand-building, he cashed in with 3 franchise models.

→Cart stalls (₹4.5-6L)
→Store models (₹20-22L)
→Flagship cafés (₹39-43L)

48 hours later: 1,609 applications.
₹200+ Cr in potential franchising revenue.

Despite global fame, Dolly still opens his original Nagpur stall daily and wears the same clothes.

When trolled about his lack of education: "If even one person with no money, no degree, no connections sees my story and believes they can build something - every insult is worth it."

From being mocked on the street to building a ₹200 Cr brand, Dolly proved that what makes you weird is often your biggest edge.

What’s your biggest takeaway from his journey?

#dollychaiwala #casestudy #entrepreneurship

content:
Bill Gates just said AI will wipe out 60% of entry-level jobs.
And honestly that's the BEST news Gen Z could have asked for.

While everyone's panicking about "AI taking jobs," smart people are using AI to skip the entry-level grind entirely.

Research tasks → gone.
Basic creative drafts → gone.
Data cleaning, formatting, admin → gone.

AI now does in minutes what junior hires used to spend hours on.

But here's what most people don't get: If AI can do the work of junior employees, you can lead a team of AI employees and use it to launch your own venture.

Instead of competing for ₹4 LPA junior roles, you can use AI to build services that companies are happy to pay ₹12 LPA for (and you can service multiple companies).

Here are 5 AI services there’s heavy demand for today:

(All easy to build with N8N or Zapier. Not sponsored.)

1. Lead qualification systems

Set up WhatsApp/email flows that auto-tag leads, score them by interest, and push hot prospects straight to sales. Companies pay $3K-8K/month for this setup. 

2. Content repurposing workflows

Turn one podcast into 10 pieces of content across platforms. Most agencies still do this manually and hate it. Automate it and charge $2K-5K per client.

3. Customer support automation

Build custom chatbots with industry context that handle 80% of tickets, escalate complex issues, and maintain conversation history. E-commerce brands desperately need this.

4. Data analysis dashboards

Connect scattered data sources into automated reports that deliver insights in real-time. What used to need a whole analytics team now takes one smart automation.

5. Cold outreach systems

Automate LinkedIn prospecting, email sequences, and follow-up tracking. One good system can 10x a company's pipeline without hiring extra Sales Development Representatives.

Gates is right - traditional entry-level work is dead. But that just means it's time to level up.

Because he companies laying off entry-level workers? They're the same ones desperately hiring AI automation consultants.

So while others compete for fewer ₹4 LPA jobs, you build your own ₹40 LPA business.

Do you think this is an opportunity for junior employees or a threat?

content:
I'm spending ₹50,000 on dinner dates - and my company is paying for it.

Before you think I've lost it, let me explain.

When NB Media was just 5 people, I knew everything about my team:

Who was homesick. Who was dating whom. Who needed an advance before rent was due.

Then we grew fast. New clients. Bigger team. Two offices.

And somewhere along the way, I became just a boss.

That's the kind of leader I never wanted to be.

So I started something called "Table Talks with CEO."

Every month, I take two random team members out for dinner:

- They pick the restaurant
- I pick up the bill, with no limit
- Rules: ZERO work talk allowed
- Just food, gossip, and vulnerability

While this is great fun for all of us, my team has also voted it my best move as CEO 😂

→ They look forward to Mondays now.

→ They text me memes instead of just project updates.

→ They've started bringing ideas to me instead of waiting for instructions.

Good vibes. No fear of hierarchy. More vulnerability.

And I can be more open with them too.

If you're a founder reading this, remember: your team is more than a resource.

Take them out for dinner. Ask about their hometown. Laugh about something stupid.

Because if you promised to build something different from any other ‘corporate’, you'll be surprised how far that one meal can go.

Have you ever done something like this with your team?

#team #leadership #entrepreneurship #growth

content:
They told these women to "go home and be mothers" instead of playing sports.
Instead, they won India's first-ever international ice hockey medal.

Here's the story that will blow your mind.

India's women's ice hockey team had:

▷ Zero government funding or sponsorship
▷ No proper training facilities
▷ Borrowed and duct-taped equipment
▷ Constant discrimination and mockery

They could’ve given up, blamed the system.

But no. They built their own solution - founding the Ladakh Women's Ice Hockey Foundation to train the next generation.

→ Half the team came from remote villages in Ladakh and Spiti, hours away from any proper sports facility.

→ They laid their own ice on frozen ponds in -15°C temperatures, working until 3 AM to build makeshift rinks.

→ They trained with hand-me-down skates and broken sticks held together with tape.

→ They practiced only after the boys finished, on worn-out surfaces.

The result?

They beat higher-ranked UAE (who had millions in funding) on their home soil, to win bronze at the Women's Asia Cup.

India's first-ever international ice hockey medal - a feat even the men's team hasn't accomplished so far.

Captain Tsewang Chuskit said hearing the national anthem wasn't just about the medal - "it was proof that we belong."

Stories like this remind me that extraordinary things happen when you stop waiting for perfect conditions and start working with what you have.

That’s how I started my journey too - and I hope this story inspires you to just get started, no matter the circumstances.

Were you inspired by this historic win too?

#sports #india #wins #history

content:
I spent ₹15 lakhs on a Goa trip with 30 friends, and my company paid for it.

30 of us. 4 days. Beaches, thalis, and one unforgettable casino night.

I know what you're thinking - "That's a lot of money for a vacation."

But here's the thing: This was a team trip. And any founder not setting aside at least ₹5L/year for a trip like this is making a huge mistake.

Because it's one of the most ROI-positive investments you can make in your team.
And before you ask - no, this wasn't VC money. We're bootstrapped. 
This came straight out of profits. Still worth every single rupee.

Because in those 4 days, I learned more about my team than I had in months of office interactions.

▷ You discover who's actually ready to lead

In the office, you're measuring output and deadlines. On a retreat, you're watching how people behave when no one's telling them what to do. Who steps up without being asked? Who checks in on the quiet ones? That's when real leadership shows up.

▷ You finally see how people actually think

Day-to-day, everyone's busy ticking off briefs and hitting targets. But when the pressure's off, that's when the magic happens. I saw who really understands storytelling, who gets audience behavior, who has genuine platform insights. You can't get this in a conference room.

▷ You align as a team, not just co-workers

Away from laptops and deadlines, we had real conversations about the business. We mapped out which content bets to make, what platforms to scale, how each person can grow. Everyone's walking into Q3 with clarity they didn't have before.

▷ You create moments that actually bond people

That spontaneous late-night casino run? We won big - not just money, but memories that no team-building exercise could create. That energy, that shared thrill, that collective high... it builds trust in a way no HR initiative ever could.

Most founders think culture happens in the office. But real culture is built when you experience unpredictable moments together.

When people see each other outside of roles and deadlines, that's when genuine trust forms.

If you've ever taken your team on a retreat, what was the one moment that made it unforgettable?

#trip #team #leadership #culture

content:
If you work in Bangalore, you'll soon be working 3 MONTHS for NO PAY.

Most people don’t understand what Karnataka’s new labour proposal really means:

1. Working hours

On paper: 10 hour workday, with 1 hour break. 
Upto 12 hours with overtime.

Reality: 
You lose 1 hour of family time every day.
You already had a lunch break, so now…
You spend 1 extra hour working for free.

An hour extra of work every workday = 260 hours / year.

2. Overtime

On paper: 144 hours of "optional" overtime per quarter. Paid double.

Reality:
- Nobody tracks overtime, so 2x pay won’t happen.
- You say no to overtime once, you lose a promotion.
- You say no twice, you're out of the company.
- You ask why - "Lack of commitment."

144 hours per quarter = 576 hours / year.

Combined, it’s the equivalent of 3 MONTHS of unpaid working hours. 

It’s ironic that India wants to compete with global economies, but we don’t learn from them.

Japan: 4-day work weeks becoming normal
Germany: Strict work-life balance laws
Denmark: 37-hour work weeks, highest productivity

Meanwhile, we’re just making it legal to exploit people.

I’ve worked in Bangalore under hellish conditions, and now it may all be legal.

But if you’re against this proposal, our voice can still make a difference.

Repost this 🔁, spread the message, share your own thoughts.

Let’s speak up against the proposal before it gets passed.

#Karnataka #LabourLaws #12HourWorkday #Overtime
